title: Playbook - Azure Logic Apps To Qualys VMDR APIs
applies_to: Logic Apps workflows that retrieve Qualys VMDR vulnerability data for reporting or downstream storage
prerequisites: Tenant platform identified from the Qualys platform-identification reference
Qualys API authentication working for both classic VM APIs and any gateway APIs needed
Decision on whether the output should come from raw detections, generated report artifacts, or both
required_permissions: Qualys account access to host detections, KnowledgeBase, and asset inventory as needed
Any report-generation permissions required by the selected report template path
request_sequence: Resolve the tenant's Qualys API server URL and gateway URL before building any Logic Apps call.
Call classic VM detection and KnowledgeBase APIs through the Logic Apps HTTP action for row-level vulnerability data.
If asset or tag scoping requires richer asset metadata, call the Global IT Asset Inventory gateway APIs separately.
If the user explicitly wants Qualys-generated report files, launch and fetch reports through the report APIs, optionally using asset tags.
Parse XML or JSON responses, normalize the fields required by downstream reporting, and persist the results to SharePoint or another store.
Handle paging, truncation, and repeated-run deduplication explicitly.
implementation_notes: Use raw detections plus KnowledgeBase plus asset inventory when the goal is Power BI-friendly tabular reporting. This is the preferred path for severity trends, tag slices, and month-over-month metrics.
Use classic report APIs only when the project specifically needs a Qualys-produced CSV, PDF, or other report artifact.
Treat the Qualys platform URL as a required input in the Logic Apps solution because pod selection changes all downstream URLs.
The Logic Apps HTTP action is sufficient here because Qualys classic APIs are HTTP-based and the main complexity is data normalization, not connector selection.
caveats: Qualys dashboard widgets are not documented as a direct API surface, so Logic Apps should derive dashboard-equivalent metrics from detections rather than expect a 'get dashboard by name' endpoint.
Classic VM APIs are XML-heavy and require explicit parsing strategy in Logic Apps or in a downstream processor.
Generated report APIs are useful, but they are less flexible than raw detections for Power BI model design.
